attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# Rendering V2 — Architectural Visualization Spec
2	
3	**Goal:** Match the visual quality of a Revit hidden-line + shaded perspective:
4	directional shadow, ambient occlusion in concave joins, crisp edge lines on
5	every element, correct PBR materials (glazing, metal, concrete, timber), and
6	real architectural geometry (door frames, window reveals, railing balusters,
7	stair treads, correct roof mesh from footprint polygon).
8	
9	**Status baseline (2026-05-06):** All geometry is Three.js `BoxGeometry` or
10	hand-coded flat `BufferGeometry` proxies. Shadows, AO, and edge lines are
11	fully absent. Directional light is at a hardcoded world position. The
12	`EffectComposer` and post-processing chain do not exist.
13	
14	**Primary file:** `packages/web/src/Viewport.tsx`  
15	**Rendering support:** `packages/web/src/viewport/materials.ts`  
16	**Camera:** `packages/web/src/viewport/cameraRig.ts`
17	
18	---
19	
20	## Phase R1 — Rendering Pipeline Foundation
21	
22	These four work packages wire the post-processing chain and enable shadow
23	maps. They are independent of geometry quality and deliver immediate visual
24	improvement on the existing box-proxy meshes.
25	
26	---
27	
28	### R1-01 · Shadow map enable
29	
30	**What the image shows:** Hard directional shadows cast by the building volume
31	onto the site slab; each element casts and receives.
32	
33	**What exists today:**  
34	`resolveLighting()` in `materials.ts` defines `shadowMapSize: 2048` but the
35	value is never read. The `DirectionalLight` is created without `castShadow`.
36	No mesh sets `castShadow` or `receiveShadow`. `renderer.shadowMap.enabled`
37	is never called.
38	
39	**What to build:**
40	
41	1. **Enable the shadow map on the renderer** immediately after construction
42	   (Viewport.tsx, near `renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer(...)`):
43	   ```ts
44	   renderer.shadowMap.enabled = true;
45	   renderer.shadowMap.type = THREE.PCFSoftShadowMap;
46	   ```
47	   `PCFSoftShadowMap` gives soft penumbra edges that match the reference
48	   image without the harsh aliasing of `BasicShadowMap`.
49	
50	2. **Configure the DirectionalLight shadow camera** when wiring the paint
51	   bundle (currently lines 689–694 of Viewport.tsx):
52	   ```ts
53	   sun.castShadow = true;
54	   sun.shadow.mapSize.set(
55	     paint.lighting.sun.shadowMapSize,   // 2048
56	     paint.lighting.sun.shadowMapSize,
57	   );
58	   // Orthographic frustum must enclose the whole building.
59	   // Use the model bounding box (recomputed on scene rebuild).
60	   const frustumHalf = Math.max(sceneRadiusM * 1.2, 20);
61	   sun.shadow.camera.left   = -frustumHalf;
62	   sun.shadow.camera.right  =  frustumHalf;
63	   sun.shadow.camera.top    =  frustumHalf;
64	   sun.shadow.camera.bottom = -frustumHalf;
65	   sun.shadow.camera.near   = 0.5;
66	   sun.shadow.camera.far    = sceneRadiusM * 4 + 50;
67	   sun.shadow.bias = -0.001;   // eliminates shadow acne on flat surfaces
68	   ```
69	   `sceneRadiusM` = half the diagonal of the scene AABB, computed after
70	   all meshes are added.
71	
72	3. **Tag every mesh** returned by the factory functions
73	   (`makeWallMesh`, `makeFloorSlabMesh`, `makeDoorMesh`, `makeWindowMesh`,
74	   `makeStairVolumeMesh`, `makeRailingMesh`, `makeRoofMassMesh`,
75	   `makeSiteMesh`):
76	   ```ts
77	   mesh.castShadow    = true;
78	   mesh.receiveShadow = true;
79	   ```
80	   Site slab should have `castShadow = false` (it sits on the ground and
81	   casts no visible shadow onto anything below it).
82	
83	4. **Recompute shadow camera frustum** on every scene rebuild (the scene is
84	   rebuilt when `elementsById` changes). After adding all meshes, compute the
85	   scene AABB and update `sun.shadow.camera.left/right/top/bottom/far`.
86	
87	**Acceptance:** The site slab receives a hard but soft-edged shadow from the
88	building mass. Walls cast shadows on each other and on the floor. The shadow
89	moves when the camera orbits (shadows are view-independent). No shadow acne
90	on flat horizontal surfaces.
91	
92	**Vitest:** Unit-test `resolveLighting()` to assert `shadowMapSize` is 2048
93	and is present (it already is). Integration coverage is via Playwright
94	screenshot diff — add a `shadow-enabled.png` baseline to
95	`e2e/ui-redesign-baselines.spec.ts`.
96	
97	---
98	
99	### R1-02 · Directional light from azimuth + elevation
100	
101	**What exists today:**  
102	`resolveLighting()` returns `{ azimuthDeg: 145, elevationDeg: 35 }` but the
103	light is placed at a hardcoded world position `(8, 12, 6)` (Viewport.tsx line
104	near `sun.position.set(8, 12, 6)`). The spec-defined azimuth and elevation
105	are never used.
106	
107	**What to build:**
108	
109	Replace the hardcoded `position.set` call with a spherical → Cartesian
110	conversion using the values from the paint bundle:
111	```ts
112	function sunPositionFromAzEl(
113	  azimuthDeg: number,
114	  elevationDeg: number,
115	  radiusM = 80,             // large enough to be "at infinity" for the scene
116	): THREE.Vector3 {
117	  const az  = THREE.MathUtils.degToRad(azimuthDeg);
118	  const el  = THREE.MathUtils.degToRad(elevationDeg);
119	  return new THREE.Vector3(
120	    radiusM * Math.cos(el) * Math.sin(az),
121	    radiusM * Math.sin(el),
122	    radiusM * Math.cos(el) * Math.cos(az),
123	  );
124	}
125	
126	sun.position.copy(sunPositionFromAzEl(
127	  paint.lighting.sun.azimuthDeg,
128	  paint.lighting.sun.elevationDeg,
129	));
130	sun.target.position.set(0, 0, 0);
131	```
132	
133	Add `azimuthDeg` and `elevationDeg` to the `LightingBundle` type in
134	`materials.ts` (they already exist in the return value of `resolveLighting()`
135	but the type does not declare them; add the fields and re-check call-sites).
136	
137	**Light theme:** azimuth 145°, elevation 35° — afternoon sun from the
138	south-west, producing a left-to-right shadow diagonal consistent with the
139	reference image.
140	
141	**Dark theme:** elevation unchanged; increase intensity to 0.6 (dark
142	background makes the same intensity look too dim).
143	
144	**Acceptance:** Shadows fall in the correct compass direction matching the
145	azimuth. Rotating the scene in walk-mode or orbit does not move the shadow
146	(shadows are world-aligned, not camera-aligned).
147	
148	---
149	
150	### R1-03 · EffectComposer + render pass chain
151	
152	**What exists today:**  
153	`renderer.render(scene, camera)` is called directly in `tick()`. No
154	`EffectComposer`, no post-processing imports.
155	
156	**What to build:**
157	
158	Install the Three.js addons (already a peer dep):
159	```ts
160	import { EffectComposer } from 'three/addons/postprocessing/EffectComposer.js';
161	import { RenderPass }     from 'three/addons/postprocessing/RenderPass.js';
162	import { OutputPass }     from 'three/addons/postprocessing/OutputPass.js';
163	```
164	
165	After renderer construction:
166	```ts
167	const composer = new EffectComposer(renderer);
168	composer.addPass(new RenderPass(scene, camera));
169	// SSAO and edge passes added by R1-04 and R1-05
170	composer.addPass(new OutputPass());
171	```
172	
173	Replace `renderer.render(scene, camera)` in `tick()` with
174	`composer.render()`.
175	
176	Resize handling: add `composer.setSize(w, h)` wherever
177	`renderer.setSize(w, h)` is called (the existing resize observer block).
178	
179	The `EffectComposer` ref must be stored alongside `renderer` in a stable ref
180	so it survives React re-renders.
181	
182	**Acceptance:** Visual output identical to baseline (since the only passes are
183	RenderPass + OutputPass). Tests: existing Playwright baselines must still
184	pass.
185	
186	---
187	
188	### R1-04 · Screen-space ambient occlusion (SSAO)
189	
190	**What exists today:**  
191	`aoIntensity: 0.4` is defined in `resolveCategoryMaterial()` and stored in
192	the material bundle but is never applied to any `MeshStandardMaterial` or any
193	post-processing pass.
194	
195	**What to build:**
196	
197	```ts
198	import { SSAOPass } from 'three/addons/postprocessing/SSAOPass.js';
199	```
200	
201	After the `RenderPass` in the composer chain:
202	```ts
203	const ssao = new SSAOPass(scene, camera, w, h);
204	ssao.kernelRadius  = 0.25;   // influence radius in world metres
205	ssao.minDistance   = 0.001;
206	ssao.maxDistance   = 0.12;
207	ssao.output        = SSAOPass.OUTPUT.Default;
208	composer.addPass(ssao);
209	```
210	
211	These values give subtle darkening in concave wall/floor junctions and eave
212	soffits without muddying the flat surfaces — matching the reference image
213	where AO is noticeable at corners but not overpowering.
214	
215	**Parameter tokens** (add to `materials.ts` `resolveLighting()`):
216	```ts
217	ssao: {
218	  kernelRadius: 0.25,
219	  minDistance: 0.001,
220	  maxDistance: 0.12,
221	}
222	```
223	
224	**Resize:** `ssao.setSize(w, h)` alongside composer resize.
225	
226	**Reduced-motion / low-power:** add a check for
227	`window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches` — if true,
228	skip the SSAO pass (`ssao.enabled = false`) to reduce GPU load.
229	
230	**Acceptance:** Wall/floor and eave/wall junctions are perceptibly darker than
231	flat surfaces. The interior soffit of the roof overhang is noticeably shaded.
232	No AO darkening appears on flat site slab in the open.
233	
234	---
235	
236	### R1-05 · Edge line pass (outline on all geometry)
237	
238	**What exists today:**  
239	No edge geometry on any mesh. Room outlines use `THREE.LineLoop`. The section
240	box cage uses `THREE.LineSegments`. Selection highlight uses `EdgesGeometry`
241	but only on the selected element.
242	
243	**What to build:**
244	
245	The reference image shows crisp 1–2 px black outlines on every architectural
246	element. There are two viable approaches; use approach A:
247	
248	**Approach A — `EdgesGeometry` overlay (correct per-mesh silhouette):**
249	
250	In each factory function, after building the main mesh, create a companion
251	`LineSegments` from `EdgesGeometry`:
252	```ts
253	function addEdges(
254	  mesh: THREE.Mesh,
255	  thresholdAngleDeg = 15,   // only edges with dihedral angle > 15°
256	  color = '#1a1a1a',
257	): THREE.LineSegments {
258	  const edges = new THREE.EdgesGeometry(mesh.geometry, thresholdAngleDeg);
259	  const mat   = new THREE.LineBasicMaterial({ color, linewidth: 1 });
260	  const lines = new THREE.LineSegments(edges, mat);
261	  lines.renderOrder = 1;   // draw on top
262	  mesh.add(lines);         // child so it inherits world transform
263	  lines.castShadow = false;
264	  lines.receiveShadow = false;
265	  return lines;
266	}
267	```
268	
269	Add `addEdges(mesh)` at the end of every factory function
270	(`makeWallMesh`, `makeFloorSlabMesh`, `makeDoorMesh`, `makeWindowMesh`,
271	`makeStairVolumeMesh`, `makeRoofMassMesh`, `makeSiteMesh`). Railing uses a
272	Group; add edges to each segment mesh within the group.
273	
274	`thresholdAngleDeg = 15` means only edges between faces meeting at >15°
275	dihedral angle get a line, suppressing internal face-splits on smooth
276	surfaces.
277	
278	**Edge color:** read from token `--color-foreground` via the existing
279	`readToken()` helper so edge lines are dark in light theme and light in dark
280	theme:
281	```ts
282	const edgeColor = readToken('--color-foreground', '#1a1a1a');
283	```
284	
285	**Approach B (alternative for later):** `OutlinePass` from
286	`three/addons/postprocessing/OutlinePass.js` — GPU-based silhouette on
287	selected objects. Use this in R3 for selection highlight instead of color
288	override. Do not use for general edge lines (it doesn't capture internal
289	edges).
290	
291	**Acceptance:** Every mesh has 1 px dark lines at dihedral angles >15°.
292	Box corners have 3 visible edges at each corner. Flat surfaces (floor top,
293	site top) have no spurious internal lines.
294	
295	---
296	
297	## Phase R2 — Geometry Accuracy
298	
299	Replace the box proxies with architecturally correct meshes. Each WP is
300	independent — they all share the `materials.ts` paint bundle and follow the
301	same pattern: factory function → `THREE.Group` containing the component meshes
302	→ `addEdges()` on each sub-mesh → `castShadow = receiveShadow = true` on all.
303	
304	---
305	
306	### R2-01 · Wall opening cuts for doors and windows
307	
308	**What exists today:**  
309	Walls are solid `BoxGeometry`. Door and window meshes are placed at the wall
310	position and approximately overlap the wall volume. There is no actual
311	geometric opening. In the reference image, wall reveals around windows and
312	doors are visible as distinct surfaces.
313	
314	**What to build:**
315	
316	Use `THREE.CSG` (constructive solid geometry) via the `three-bvh-csg` package
317	(add to devDeps: `pnpm add three-bvh-csg`) to subtract door and window
318	volumes from the wall.
319	
320	```ts
321	import { Brush, Evaluator, SUBTRACTION } from 'three-bvh-csg';
322	
323	function makeWallWithOpenings(
324	  wall: WallElem,
325	  hostedElements: (DoorElem | WindowElem)[],
326	  elevM: number,
327	  paint: ViewportPaintBundle | null,
328	): THREE.Mesh {
329	  // 1. Build the solid wall brush (same geometry as makeWallMesh today)
330	  const wallBrush = new Brush(wallGeometry, wallMaterial);
331	  wallBrush.updateMatrixWorld();
332	
333	  // 2. For each hosted door/window, build a cutter brush
334	  for (const hosted of hostedElements) {
335	    const cutterGeom  = hosted.kind === 'door'
336	      ? doorCutterGeometry(hosted, wall)
337	      : windowCutterGeometry(hosted, wall);
338	    const cutterBrush = new Brush(cutterGeom, wallMaterial);
339	    cutterBrush.updateMatrixWorld();
340	    const evaluator = new Evaluator();
341	    wallBrush.geometry = evaluator.evaluate(
342	      wallBrush, cutterBrush, SUBTRACTION
343	    ).geometry;
344	  }
345	  const result = new THREE.Mesh(wallBrush.geometry, wallMaterial);
346	  result.userData.bimPickId = wall.id;
347	  return result;
348	}
349	```
350	
351	**Cutter volumes:**
352	- Door cutter: same `(width, height, thickness + 0.1)` box as today's door
353	  proxy, placed at `hostedXZ(door, wall)` at `elevM` (floor level) so the
354	  cut goes to the sill.
355	- Window cutter: `(width, height, thickness + 0.1)` box placed at
356	  `elevM + sillHeight`.
357	
358	**Performance:** CSG is O(n·m) in polygon count. Gate it behind a
359	`ENABLE_CSG` flag (`import.meta.env.VITE_ENABLE_CSG === 'true'`); default
360	off until R2-02 and R2-03 deliver the frame/reveal geometry that makes the
361	cuts visible and worth the cost.
362	
363	**Acceptance:** Looking at a wall face-on, the door and window openings are
364	visible as holes through the wall. The wall edges around each opening are
365	visible as separate geometry lines from `EdgesGeometry`. Interior wall
366	reveal surfaces are a separate face receiving AO.
367	
368	---
369	
370	### R2-02 · Door frame + panel geometry
371	
372	**What exists today:**  
373	`makeDoorMesh` returns a single `BoxGeometry(width, height, depth)` with the
374	wall material color. No frame, no panel, no swing arc.
375	
376	**What to build:**
377	
378	Replace with a `THREE.Group` containing:
379	
380	```
381	DoorGroup
382	  ├── frame        THREE.Group
383	  │   ├── head     BoxGeometry(frameWidth + 2·jamb, frameSect, depth)
384	  │   ├── jamb-L   BoxGeometry(frameSect, frameWidth, depth)
385	  │   └── jamb-R   BoxGeometry(frameSect, frameWidth, depth)
386	  ├── panel        BoxGeometry(leafWidth, leafHeight, panelThick)
387	  └── threshold    BoxGeometry(leafWidth, 0.02, depth)
388	```
389	
390	**Dimensions** (all in metres, derived from `DoorElem` fields):
391	```
392	leafWidth    = clamp(door.widthMm  / 1000, 0.35, 4)
393	leafHeight   = clamp(door.heightMm / 1000, 0.6, 2.5)
394	                     — use door.heightMm if present; else wall.heightMm * 0.86
395	panelThick   = 0.045   // 45 mm solid core leaf
396	depth        = clamp(wall.thicknessMm / 1000, 0.08, 0.5)
397	frameSect    = 0.07    // 70 mm frame section width
398	jambDepth    = depth   // jambs flush with wall face
399	```
400	
401	**Materials:**
402	- Frame and threshold: `door` category material (brown)
403	- Panel: `door` category with slightly higher roughness (0.75 vs 0.6) to
404	  read as painted timber vs. bare frame
405	
406	**Swing arc** (optional, deferred to R2-X): A `THREE.CircleGeometry`
407	quarter-arc flat on the floor plane at `elevM`, showing the door swing
408	direction based on `door.handingL` boolean (`true` = left-hinge). Can be
409	added as a `LineLoop` at low cost.
410	
411	**Acceptance:** Door units look like door frames with a panel leaf, not
412	floating boxes. Frame lines are visible as `EdgesGeometry` lines. Panel has
413	correct depth visible from side view.
414	
415	---
416	
417	### R2-03 · Window frame + glazing pane geometry
418	
419	**What exists today:**  
420	`makeWindowMesh` returns `BoxGeometry(width, height, depth)` with 84%
421	opacity. No frame, no glazing pane, no reveal.
422	
423	**What to build:**
424	
425	Replace with a `THREE.Group`:
426	```
427	WindowGroup
428	  ├── frame        THREE.Group (4 members: head, sill, jamb-L, jamb-R)
429	  │   ├── head     BoxGeometry(width, frameSect, depth)
430	  │   ├── sill     BoxGeometry(width, frameSect, depth)
431	  │   ├── jamb-L   BoxGeometry(frameSect, height, depth)
432	  │   └── jamb-R   BoxGeometry(frameSect, height, depth)
433	  ├── glazing      BoxGeometry(glazingW, glazingH, 0.006)
434	  └── mullion      BoxGeometry(frameSect, glazingH, 0.012)   [if width > 1.2m]
435	```
436	
437	**Dimensions:**
438	```
439	outerW     = clamp(win.widthMm  / 1000, 0.14, 4)
440	outerH     = clamp(win.heightMm / 1000, 0.05, 3.5)
441	frameSect  = 0.06     // 60 mm frame profile
442	depth      = clamp(wall.thicknessMm / 1000 + 0.02, 0.06, 0.5)
443	glazingW   = outerW - 2 * frameSect
444	glazingH   = outerH - 2 * frameSect
445	```
446	
447	**Glazing material** (new category, or use `window`):
448	```ts
449	{
450	  color:       readToken('--cat-glazing', '#c8d8ea'),
451	  roughness:   0.05,     // near-mirror glass
452	  metalness:   0.0,
453	  opacity:     0.35,     // more transparent than the current 0.84 proxy
454	  transparent: true,
455	  envMapIntensity: 1.0,  // enabled once R3-05 adds env map
456	  side: THREE.DoubleSide,
457	}
458	```
459	
460	**Mullion:** if `outerW > 1.2m` add a vertical divider at centre; if
461	`outerH > 1.5m` add a horizontal transom as well.
462	
463	**Reveal faces:** The space between the glazing plane and the outer wall
464	face is visible in the reference image as a set-back reveal. This is
465	automatically present if R2-01 cuts the opening: the inner wall faces
466	become the reveal depth.
467	
468	**Acceptance:** Windows render as frame + inset glazing pane. Glazing is
469	clearly more transparent (0.35 opacity) than walls. Edge lines show the
470	frame sections separately from the glass.
471	
472	---
473	
474	### R2-04 · Stair tread + riser solid geometry
475	
476	**What exists today:**  
477	`makeStairVolumeMesh` returns a single `BoxGeometry(run, rise, width)`
478	bounding volume proxy. It does not model individual treads.
479	
480	**What to build:**
481	
482	Replace with a `THREE.Group` containing one `BoxGeometry` per tread:
483	
484	```ts
485	function makeStairTreadGroup(
486	  stair: StairElem,
487	  elementsById: Record<string, Element>,
488	  paint: ViewportPaintBundle | null,
489	): THREE.Group {
490	  const group = new THREE.Group();
491	
492	  // Resolve level elevations
493	  const baseLevelElev  = elevationMForLevel(stair.baseLevelId, elementsById);
494	  const topLevelElev   = elevationMForLevel(stair.topLevelId,  elementsById);
495	  const totalRise      = Math.abs(topLevelElev - baseLevelElev);
496	  const riserCount     = Math.max(
497	    stair.riserCount ?? Math.round(totalRise / (stair.riserMm ?? 175) * 1000),
498	    2,
499	  );
500	  const riserH         = totalRise / riserCount;
501	  const runStart       = { x: stair.runStartMm.xMm / 1000, z: stair.runStartMm.yMm / 1000 };
502	  const runEnd         = { x: stair.runEndMm.xMm   / 1000, z: stair.runEndMm.yMm   / 1000 };
503	  const runLen         = Math.hypot(runEnd.x - runStart.x, runEnd.z - runStart.z);
504	  const treadDepth     = runLen / riserCount;
505	  const stairWidth     = clamp(stair.widthMm / 1000, 0.3, 4);
506	  const angle          = Math.atan2(runEnd.z - runStart.z, runEnd.x - runStart.x);
507	  const treadThick     = 0.040;  // 40 mm tread thickness
508	
509	  for (let i = 0; i < riserCount; i++) {
510	    const treadGeom = new THREE.BoxGeometry(treadDepth, treadThick, stairWidth);
511	    const treadMesh = new THREE.Mesh(treadGeom, stairMat(paint));
512	    // Position: advance along run, step up
513	    const cx = runStart.x + (i + 0.5) / riserCount * (runEnd.x - runStart.x);
514	    const cz = runStart.z + (i + 0.5) / riserCount * (runEnd.z - runStart.z);
515	    const cy = baseLevelElev + (i + 0.5) * riserH + treadThick / 2;
516	    treadMesh.position.set(cx, cy, cz);
517	    treadMesh.rotation.y = angle;
518	    treadMesh.castShadow = treadMesh.receiveShadow = true;
519	    treadMesh.userData.bimPickId = stair.id;
520	    addEdges(treadMesh);
521	    group.add(treadMesh);
522	  }
523	
524	  // Stringer (solid side plate)
525	  const stringerH    = totalRise;
526	  const stringerGeom = new THREE.BoxGeometry(runLen, stringerH, 0.025);
527	  // ... position at run midpoint, half the total rise
528	  // Two stringers: left side and right side (offset ±stairWidth/2 along perp)
529	  group.add(leftStringer, rightStringer);
530	
531	  group.userData.bimPickId = stair.id;
532	  return group;
533	}
534	```
535	
536	**Element fields used:** `runStartMm`, `runEndMm`, `widthMm`, `baseLevelId`,
537	`topLevelId`, `riserCount`, `riserMm`.
538	
539	**Acceptance:** A staircase renders as individual stacked tread boxes with
540	correct step-up progression from base level to top level. Shadow falls from
541	upper treads onto lower treads. Count of treads matches `riserCount`.
542

596	### R2-06 · Roof from footprint polygon with true gable/hip mesh
597	
598	**What exists today:**  
599	`makeRoofMassMesh` builds a custom `BufferGeometry` with hand-coded positions
600	for a single-ridge gable, but only when the bounding box has a clear dominant
601	axis. L-shaped and hip footprints are not handled. The ridge always runs along
602	the longer axis regardless of `ridge_axis` field. Overhang is an XZ extension
603	of the AABB, not a true polygon offset.
604	
605	**What to build:**
606	
607	**Stage 1 — Gable pitched rectangle (fix current):**
608	
609	The existing approach is mostly correct for rectangular footprints. Fix the
610	following:
611	- Use `roof.slopeDeg` (already read), but also respect `roof.ridgeAxis`
612	  (`'x'` | `'z'` | null). If `ridgeAxis` is null, choose the longer bounding
613	  box axis.
614	- Overhang: offset the footprint polygon outward by `overhangMm / 1000` using
615	  a proper polygon offset (not AABB expansion). A simple parallel offset for
616	  convex polygons: shift each edge outward by `overhangMm / 1000` and
617	  intersect adjacent offset edges. Implement as `offsetPolygonMm(pts, dist)`.
618	- Eave plate height: already uses `max(wall heights at reference level)` —
619	  keep this.
620	
621	**Stage 2 — Hip roof (new):**
622	
623	A hip roof has four sloped faces converging to a ridge point (for square
624	footprint) or ridge line (for rectangular). Algorithm:
625	
626	```ts
627	function makeHipRoofMesh(footprintMm: [number, number][], slopeDeg: number,
628	                          overhangMm: number, eaveElevM: number): BufferGeometry {
629	  // 1. Offset footprint outward by overhangMm
630	  const outer = offsetPolygonMm(footprintMm, overhangMm);
631	  // 2. Compute hip-line intersections using the slope angle
632	  //    Each eave edge projects inward at slopeDeg until edges meet
633	  //    Standard hip: use the "equal hip" algorithm — bisect each corner,
634	  //    advance along bisector at tan(slopeRad) * inset
635	  // 3. Ridge line = locus of convergence points
636	  // 4. Build faces: 4 trapezia (long sides) + 4 triangles (hips at corners)
637	  //    or 2 trapezia + 2 gables for ridged hip
638	  // ...
639	}
640	```
641	
642	**Stage 3 — L-shape gable (new):**
643	
644	Detect L-shaped footprints (convex hull area / polygon area < 0.85). Split
645	the L into two overlapping rectangles along the primary recess. Build a gable
646	mesh for each rectangle and merge with `BufferGeometryUtils.mergeGeometries`.
647	The valley at the junction is a separate face.
648	
649	**Acceptance (Stage 1):** Rectangular footprint gable renders with correct
650	ridge direction per `ridgeAxis`, correct eave plate height, and an overhang
651	that offsets the polygon (not just the bounding box).
652	
653	**Acceptance (Stage 2):** Seed house V2 roof (35° gable per
654	`seed-house-v2-commands.mjs`) renders correctly from all four elevations.
655	
656	**Acceptance (Stage 3):** An L-shaped house plan (e.g. the V2 seed house
657	wing) renders a hipped valley at the junction.
658

661	### R2-07 · Floor slab from boundary polygon
662	
663	**What exists today:**  
664	`makeFloorSlabMesh` uses `xzBoundsMm(floor.boundaryMm)` — computes the AABB
665	of the boundary polygon and creates a `BoxGeometry(spanX, thickness, spanZ)`.
666	For L-shaped and irregular floor plans this is a gross approximation
667	(the bounding box overfills).
668	
669	**What to build:**
670	
671	Replace the `BoxGeometry` with `THREE.ExtrudeGeometry` from the actual
672	boundary polygon:
673	```ts
674	function makeFloorSlabMesh(floor: FloorElem, ...): THREE.Mesh {
675	  const shape = new THREE.Shape();
676	  const pts   = floor.boundaryMm;
677	
678	  // BIM coordinate convention: boundaryMm is XY in plan space
679	  // Three.js extrude works in XY, we rotate to XZ afterwards
680	  shape.moveTo(pts[0][0] / 1000, pts[0][1] / 1000);
681	  for (let i = 1; i < pts.length; i++) shape.lineTo(pts[i][0] / 1000, pts[i][1] / 1000);
682	  shape.closePath();
683	
684	  const thick = clamp(floor.thicknessMm / 1000, 0.05, 1.8);
685	  const geom  = new THREE.ExtrudeGeometry(shape, { depth: thick, bevelEnabled: false });
686	  // Rotate so the extruded axis is vertical (Y up), footprint is in XZ
687	  geom.rotateX(-Math.PI / 2);
688	
689	  const elevM = elevationMForLevel(floor.levelId, elementsById);
690	  const mesh  = new THREE.Mesh(geom, floorMat(paint));
691	  mesh.position.y = elevM - thick;   // underside at level elevation
692	  mesh.castShadow = mesh.receiveShadow = true;
693	  mesh.userData.bimPickId = floor.id;
694	  addEdges(mesh, 20);   // 20° threshold to suppress internal triangulation edges
695	  return mesh;
696	}
697	```
698	
699	**Winding order:** `floor.boundaryMm` points are CCW when viewed from above
700	in plan space (standard BIM convention). `THREE.Shape` expects CCW. No
701	reversal needed.
702	
703	**Slab openings:** `slab_opening` elements have a `hostFloorId` reference.
704	When a slab opening exists, use it as a hole in the `THREE.Shape`:
705	```ts
706	const opening = slabOpenings.find(o => o.hostFloorId === floor.id);
707	if (opening) {
708	  const hole = new THREE.Path();
709	  hole.moveTo(...); // opening.boundaryMm
710	  shape.holes.push(hole);
711	}
712	```
713	
714	**Acceptance:** L-shaped floor (V2 seed house ground floor) renders as the
715	correct L silhouette, not a filled bounding box. The shadow cast by walls on
716	the floor follows the actual floor boundary. Stair opening appears as a hole
717	in the floor slab.
718

722	
723	**What exists today:**  
724	`makeSiteMesh` already uses `THREE.ExtrudeGeometry` from `site.boundaryMm`.
725	This is mostly correct. Two gaps:
726	
727	1. Thickness is read as `site.padThicknessMm ?? 150 / 1000` — the operator
728	   precedence is wrong: `150 / 1000 = 0.15` evaluates before `??`, so the
729	   nullish fallback is `0.15` m (fine), but if `padThicknessMm` is `0` this
730	   will return `0` not the default. Fix:
731	   ```ts
732	   const thick = clamp((site.padThicknessMm ?? 150) / 1000, 0.05, 2);
733	   ```

735	2. Site has no `castShadow` (intentional, the site is the shadow receiver, not
736	   caster). Confirm `receiveShadow = true` is set.
737	
738	3. Site material: add `aoMapIntensity: 0` to prevent SSAO from darkening the
739	   exposed site plane (SSAO on a large flat plane can produce ugly banding
740	   artefacts near the edges of the building footprint).
741	
742	**Acceptance:** Site renders at correct thickness. Building mass casts a
743	shadow onto the site slab. No AO banding artefact on the flat site surface.

792	### R3-02 · Glazing material with environment map
793	
794	**What exists today:**  
795	Window mesh uses `window` category material with `opacity: 0.84`. No
796	reflection, no IBL.
797	
798	**What to build:**
799	
800	Add an equirectangular environment map for image-based lighting. Use a
801	lightweight procedural sky texture (no HDR download required):
802	
803	```ts
804	import { Sky } from 'three/addons/objects/Sky.js';
805	
806	function buildEnvMap(
807	  renderer: THREE.WebGLRenderer,
808	  scene: THREE.Scene,
809	  sunAzimuthDeg: number,
810	  sunElevationDeg: number,
811	): THREE.Texture {
812	  const sky    = new Sky();
813	  sky.scale.setScalar(450000);
814	  const uniforms = sky.material.uniforms;
815	  uniforms['turbidity'].value    = 3;
816	  uniforms['rayleigh'].value     = 0.5;
817	  uniforms['mieCoefficient'].value  = 0.005;
818	  uniforms['mieDirectionalG'].value = 0.8;
819	
820	  const phi   = THREE.MathUtils.degToRad(90 - sunElevationDeg);
821	  const theta = THREE.MathUtils.degToRad(sunAzimuthDeg);
822	  const sunPos = new THREE.Vector3().setFromSphericalCoords(1, phi, theta);
823	  uniforms['sunPosition'].value.copy(sunPos);
824	
825	  // Render sky into a PMREMGenerator cube
826	  scene.add(sky);
827	  const pmrem   = new THREE.PMREMGenerator(renderer);
828	  const envMap  = pmrem.fromScene(sky as unknown as THREE.Scene).texture;
829	  scene.remove(sky);
830	  pmrem.dispose();
831	  return envMap;
832	}
833	```
834	
835	Apply `envMap` to the scene (`scene.environment = envMap`) and specifically
836	to the glazing material with `envMapIntensity: 1.2`.
837	
838	Rebuild the env map whenever the theme changes (light/dark changes sky colour
839	via `sky.material.uniforms['rayleigh']`).
840	
841	**Acceptance:** Window glazing shows a subtle sky reflection. Dark areas of
842	the sky are visible in the glazing when the camera is at a shallow angle.
843	Roughness 0.05 makes the reflection sharp but faint.

847	### R3-03 · Cladding surface detail
848	
849	**What the image shows:** Vertical board-on-board cladding visible as parallel
850	vertical lines on the wall faces. This is a material/geometry detail.
851	
852	**What to build — geometry option (preferred for edge-line rendering):**
853	
854	For walls tagged with `wall.material === 'timber_cladding'` (or equivalent
855	category marker), add a `CladdingGroup` as a child of the wall mesh:
856	
857	```ts
858	function addCladdingBoards(
859	  wallMesh: THREE.Mesh,
860	  wallLen: number,
861	  wallH: number,
862	  boardWidthMm = 120,    // 120 mm board face width
863	  gapMm = 10,            // 10 mm shadow gap
864	): void {
865	  const pitchM = (boardWidthMm + gapMm) / 1000;
866	  const count  = Math.floor(wallLen / pitchM);
867	  const boardGeom = new THREE.BoxGeometry(0.012, wallH - 0.05, pitchM - 0.01);
868	  const boardMat  = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({
869	    color: wallMesh.material.color,
870	    roughness: 0.85,
871	    metalness: 0,
872	  });
873	  for (let i = 0; i < count; i++) {
874	    const board = new THREE.Mesh(boardGeom, boardMat);
875	    board.position.set(
876	      0,                      // centred on wall (local X is wall face)
877	      0,                      // centred on wall height
878	      -wallLen / 2 + (i + 0.5) * pitchM,
879	    );
880	    board.castShadow = board.receiveShadow = true;
881	    wallMesh.add(board);
882	  }
883	}
884	```
885	
886	**Texture option (fallback for walls not tagged with cladding):**
887	
888	For untextured walls, a `NormalMap` with 2 mm vertical groove pattern can be
889	baked as a 512×512 canvas texture and applied to the wall material:
890	```ts
891	wallMat.normalMap = buildGrooveNormalMap(boardWidthMm, gapMm);
892	wallMat.normalScale.set(0.4, 0.4);
893	```
894	
895	This is lower fidelity but has no polygon overhead.
896	
897	**Acceptance:** Walls tagged as timber cladding show clearly visible vertical
898	board lines in the 3D view. Individual board shadows are visible on the wall
899	face with shadow maps enabled.

948	## Phase R4 — Section Perspective View
949	
950	The reference image appears to be a **section perspective**: the building is
951	sliced by a cutting plane to reveal the interior.
952	
953	---
954	
955	### R4-01 · Section cut interior reveal
956	
957	**What exists today:**  
958	`SectionBox` controller (`viewport/sectionBox.ts`) defines six clipping
959	planes and `SectionBox.clippingPlanes()` returns them. The planes are applied
960	per-material in the renderer. The interior surfaces exposed by the cut are the
961	raw cross-sections of `BoxGeometry` faces.
962	
963	**What exists currently in the scene:** When the section box clips a wall,
964	the interior of the wall `BoxGeometry` face is empty (the geometry has no
965	interior face at the cut plane). The exposed cross-section is invisible — you
966	see through the wall to the geometry behind.
967	
968	**What to build:**
969	
970	Use Three.js `Plane` stencil rendering to cap the section cut:
971	
972	```ts
973	// For each clip plane, render a stencil-masked plane cap
974	// Standard Three.js clipping plane cap technique:
975	// 1. Render scene normally with clipping planes + stencil write
976	// 2. Render a full-screen quad masked by stencil for the cap faces
977	```
978	
979	Implementation using the standard Three.js clipping cap demo pattern:
980	
981	```ts
982	function buildClipPlaneCap(
983	  plane: THREE.Plane,
984	  capColor: string,
985	): THREE.Mesh {
986	  const capGeom = new THREE.PlaneGeometry(500, 500);
987	  const capMat  = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({
988	    color: capColor,
989	    roughness: 0.9,
990	    metalness: 0,
991	    side: THREE.DoubleSide,
992	    // Stencil: only render where stencil buffer says "clipped"
993	    stencilWrite: true,
994	    stencilRef: 1,
995	    stencilFunc: THREE.EqualStencilFunc,
996	    stencilFail: THREE.KeepStencilOp,
997	    stencilZFail: THREE.KeepStencilOp,
998	    stencilZPass: THREE.ReplaceStencilOp,
999	  });
1000	  const cap = new THREE.Mesh(capGeom, capMat);
1001	  // Orient the cap mesh to face along the plane normal
1002	  cap.onBeforeRender = (_r, _s, cam) => {
1003	    cap.lookAt(cam.position);
1004	    cap.position.copy(plane.normal).multiplyScalar(-plane.constant);
1005	  };
1006	  return cap;
1007	}
1008	```
1009	
1010	Cap color: use `--color-surface-strong` (slightly off-white) to match the
1011	Revit cut-face convention (lighter than the element body).
1012	
1013	**Section perspective UI:** Add a "Section perspective" button in the
1014	`Viewport3DLayersPanel` (built in T-11) that activates a half-space clip at
1015	the active section plane defined by the first `section_cut` element. This is
1016	distinct from the full 6-plane section box.
1017	
1018	**Acceptance:** In section perspective mode, the building is sliced by a
1019	vertical plane parallel to the section cut line defined in the project. The
1020	cut face is capped in off-white. Interior spaces (floor slabs, interior
1021	walls, stair) are visible through the cut. Edge lines from `EdgesGeometry`
1022	appear on the cap face cross-sections.
1023	
1024	---
1025	
1026	### R4-02 · Ortho / parallel projection toggle
1027	
1028	**What the image shows:** The reference image uses a perspective projection,
1029	but Revit section views typically offer both perspective and parallel (ortho).
1030	
1031	**What to build:**
1032	
1033	Add an orthographic camera option alongside the perspective camera. The
1034	`CameraRig` already tracks `target`, `radius`, `azimuth`, `elevation`.
1035	Add an `orthoMode` boolean to `CameraRigState`:
1036	
1037	```ts
1038	// cameraRig.ts
1039	orthoCamera(): THREE.OrthographicCamera {
1040	  const snap = this.snapshot();
1041	  const halfH = this.state.radius * Math.tan(THREE.MathUtils.degToRad(27.5));
1042	  // 27.5° = half of default 55° fov
1043	  return new THREE.OrthographicCamera(
1044	    -halfH * aspect, halfH * aspect,
1045	    halfH, -halfH,
1046	    0.05, 500,
1047	  );
1048	}
1049	```
1050	
1051	In `Viewport.tsx`: hold both cameras. Switch active camera on a toggle button
1052	in the 3D viewport HUD (small icon button, top-right of canvas). The
1053	`EffectComposer`'s `RenderPass` and all passes that receive `camera` must
1054	be updated to point at the active camera.
1055	
1056	**Acceptance:** Toggle between perspective (default) and orthographic. Ortho
1057	mode shows parallel projection — distant walls appear the same size as close
1058	walls. Useful for elevation and section views.

1067	   - Directional shadow from south-west at 35° elevation cast on site slab

1070	   - Edge lines on every architectural element at 1 px

1072	   - Window frames with glazing pane set into reveals
